sources.notes: Color exactly k tree edges and minimize the maximum number of colors on any path, for all k.
Official tutorial inverts the answer: for fixed inconvenience d, maximize colors by peeling outer leaf levels and using a diameter-midpoint structure.

annotation.summary: For every number of edge colors k, minimize the maximum number of distinct colors appearing on any tree path.
annotation.constraints: Total n is 3e5, so answers for all k must be produced in O(n log n) or O(n).
annotation.core_idea: Invert the query: for a fixed diameter-like inconvenience d, maximize the number of connected color components. Every color can be assumed connected, and the optimum has a center/midpoint structure. For odd d, remove the outermost (d-1)/2 leaf layers; for even d, remove d/2-1 leaf layers and use the maximum degree vertex in the remaining core. A leaf-peeling process maintains remaining degrees and writes the best d for each achievable number of colors, then suffix-minimizes the answer array.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) or O(n) per test case.
tricks: leaf peeling from the outside inward

solution_variants.name: leaf-peeling-inverted-answer
solution_variants.summary: Peel leaves layer by layer, track remaining maximum degree, and record candidate inconvenience values for color counts.

tags.evidence: The whole structure is a tree coloring problem and the solution depends on tree centers, leaves, and degrees.
The optimal construction pushes colors outward and peels the outer leaf layers greedily for each diameter bound.
The final all-k answer is produced by writing best values for achievable color counts and suffix-minimizing them.
The proof describes an optimal coloring shape with connected components around a center/core.
